coredata.dc:title: Detection and adsorption of 2-methyl-4-chlorophenoxyacetic acid in vegetables via dual-functional molecularly imprinted polymer doping with carbon dot
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haiping
Xu, Yi
Bai, Qingyan
Ma, Shujuan
Bo, Chunmiao
Ou, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: 2-Methyl-4-chlorophenoxyacetic acid (MCPA) is one of the most widely used herbicides, so adsorption and detection of MCPA in the environment is critical. Blue fluorescent carbon dot (CD) was synthesized from citric acid and urea, which could be quenched by MCPA. Herein, bifunctional molecularly imprinted polymer (CD@MIP) was prepared on monodisperse poly (glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) microspheres, with 4-vinylpyridine as the functional monomer, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linking agent, and doped with CD. The enrichment ability of CD@MIP for MCPA and fluorescence detection performance were determined. The maximum adsorption amount of MCPA was 93.9 mg g−1 as determined by isothermal adsorption experiments and was in accordance with the Langmuir adsorption model. The results of the kinetic experiments showed that the adsorption equilibrium reached within 30 min, which possessed a relatively fast adsorption rate and was in accordance with the pseudo-second-order adsorption model. Both MIP without CD and non-imprinted polymers were also fabricated and tested as references. Fluorescence experiments showed good linearity of CD@MIP in the range of 0–80 μmol. The cabbage samples were analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography with a linear range of 0.02–15 μg mL−1, recoveries of 90.5%–98% and low relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) of 1.5%–5.9%. CD@MIP with excellent performance provides a feasible practical application in the detection and enrichment of MCPA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
2-Methyl-4-chlorophenoxyacetic acid
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Fluorescence
High performance liquid chromatography

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of MCPA in real samples by HPLC
Analysis of CD@MIP in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support is gratefully acknowledged from the CAS-Weigao Research & Development Program ([2017]-009) and the National Natural Sciences Foundation of Ningxia (No. 2021AAC02017) to J. Ou.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Process flow for preparation of CD@MIP via SI-ATRP technique.
SEM images of monodisperse (A, B) poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres, (C, D) MIP and (E, F) CD@MIP.
(A) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm, (B) pore size distribution, (C) FT-IR spectra and (D) TG analysis.
(A) Isothermal and (B) kinetic adsorption profiles of MIP, CD@MIP and NIP.
(A, C) Fluorescence spectra and (B, D) Stern-Volmer curves of (A, B) CD and (C, D) CD@MIP quenching by MCPA.
Chromatograms of (A) standard sample of MCPA, (B) actual cabbage samples and (C) actual cabbage samples enriched by CD@MIP.
Comparison of specific surface area, pore volume and average pore diameter of poly (GMA-co-EDMA), CD@MIP and MIP.
Comparison of MCPA Adsorbent Materials in this Study with Other Reports.

head.ce:title: Detection and adsorption of 2-methyl-4-chlorophenoxyacetic acid in vegetables via dual-functional molecularly imprinted polymer doping with carbon dot

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory for Chemical Engineering and Technology, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, North Minzu University, Yinchuan 750021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory for Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710127, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710127, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710127, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2-Methyl-4-chlorophenoxyacetic acid (MCPA) is one of the most widely used herbicides, so adsorption and detection of MCPA in the environment is critical. Blue fluorescent carbon dot (CD) was synthesized from citric acid and urea, which could be quenched by MCPA. Herein, bifunctional molecularly imprinted polymer (CD@MIP) was prepared on monodisperse poly (glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) microspheres, with 4-vinylpyridine as the functional monomer, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linking agent, and doped with CD. The enrichment ability of CD@MIP for MCPA and fluorescence detection performance were determined. The maximum adsorption amount of MCPA was 93.9 mg g−1 as determined by isothermal adsorption experiments and was in accordance with the Langmuir adsorption model. The results of the kinetic experiments showed that the adsorption equilibrium reached within 30 min, which possessed a relatively fast adsorption rate and was in accordance with the pseudo-second-order adsorption model. Both MIP without CD and non-imprinted polymers were also fabricated and tested as references. Fluorescence experiments showed good linearity of CD@MIP in the range of 0–80 μmol. The cabbage samples were analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography with a linear range of 0.02–15 μg mL−1, recoveries of 90.5%–98% and low relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) of 1.5%–5.9%. CD@MIP with excellent performance provides a feasible practical application in the detection and enrichment of MCPA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenoxyacid herbicides have been widely utilized in agriculture for controlling weeds in agricultural fields due to their high selectivity and potent herbicidal activity [1,2]. Among them, 2-methyl-4-chlorophenoxyacetic acid (MCPA) is one of the most widely used herbicides. Owing to the high solubility of MCPA in water and low adsorption in soil [3], these herbicides can easily contaminate surface water and groundwater on account of large amounts of use as they infiltrate with rivers and soils [4], posing a serious safety hazard to human health and the ecological environment [5,6]. Therefore, the way of adsorption and detection of MCPA in the environment is critical.

Currently, various methods for the removal of MCPA have been developed, such as membrane filtration, ion exchange, chemical oxidation, biodegradation and adsorption [7]. Among them, adsorption is considered the most practical and promising technique attributed to its low cost, high selectivity, and reusability of adsorbent materials [8]. Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) is polymeric material with special structures and properties, which have the advantages of high selectivity, high stability, low cost and reusability, and can be used for selective extraction and cleanup of target analytes [9,10]. Guć prepared MIP based on MCPA as a template by precipitation polymerization, which were applied in MCPA determination with flowing atmospheric-pressure afterglow mass spectrometry technique [11]. Meseguer-Lloret synthesized magnetic MIP in nanometers using MCPA as a template molecule for magnetic solid-phase extraction (SPE), and water samples from different sources were analyzed with high recovery of 98% [12]. Zanoni synthesized a MIP screen-printed cell pack for detection of phenoxy herbicides using MCPA as a template, and experimentally demonstrated excellent detection performance of the sensor for phenoxyacetic acid herbicides [13]. Therefore, MIP is a good choice as an extractant for SPE. However, traditional MIP preparation methods have the disadvantages of incomplete elution of template molecules and easy leakage. Surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) offers advantages, including adaptable interfacial control, precise modification, high efficiency, and ease application [14]. The MIP prepared by SI-ATRP technique not only can effectively avoid the leakage of the template molecule MCPA, but also is more efficiently to elute MCPA. The imprinted sites overlaid the surface of the adsorbent material, which is more advantageous for the adsorption of MCPA.

Carbon dot (CD) is a new class of nanomaterials derived from carbon materials that have attracted much attention since their discovery [15–20]. Owing to excellent photoluminescence properties, high biocompatibility, easy and green synthesis route, better water solubility, better photostability, and tunable surface functionality, CD have been widely used for high-sensitivity detection in biosensing [21,22]. It is foreseeable that the combination of CD and molecular imprinting technology can prepare dual-functional MIP with both photoluminescence and adsorption capabilities. Our group [23–27] has done a few researches on CD-based bifunctional materials, and incorporation of MIP with CD was developed to prepare bifunctional imprinted materials with fluorescence detection and selective adsorption.

In our case, a composite CD@MIP was prepared by SI-ATRP technique using monodisperse poly (glycerol methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) (poly (GMA-co-EDMA)) microspheres as a matrix, MCPA as a template molecule, 4-vinylpyridine (4-VP) as a functional monomer, and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EDMA) as a cross-linker. The adsorption capacity and properties of the materials were investigated by isothermal adsorption experiments and kinetic adsorption experiments. The fluorescence properties of the materials were determined by fluorescence experiments, and the enrichment ability of the materials on MCPA in real samples was also analyzed by HPLC. As a comparison, the properties of MIP without added CD and non-imprinted polymer (NIP) were also tested.

Glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), EDMA, 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile, urea, citric acid and polyvinyl alcohol were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). MCPA, terephthalic acid (TPA), 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), 4-VP, 2-2′-bipyridine (Bpy), 2-bromoisobutyryl bromide, sulfuric acid, glacial acetic acid, ethanol, sulfuric acid, glacial acetic acid, ethanol, disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) and copper bromide (CuBr) were from Enocet (Beijing, China). Phosphoric acid and chromatographic methanol were purchased from Daimao (Tianjin, China).

The blue fluorescent CD was synthesized based on previous reports [28]. Typically, 0.1 g of urea and 0.21 g of citric acid were added to a PTFE reactor containing 25 mL of water. After ultrasonic dissolvation, the reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 4 h. Then, after the reactor was removed and naturally cooled to room temperature, the product was obtained by filtration through a polyethersulfone microporous membrane (0.22 μm). The filtrate was then placed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da), dialyzed in deionized water for 48 h and filtered again through a polyethersulfone microporous membrane (0.22 μm). The results CD was concentrated by rotary evaporation.

First, monodisperse poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were prepared according to our previous method [29]. The prepared monodisperse poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were then modified. Three grams of monodisperse poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were accurately weighed and dispersed in 0.1 mol L−1 sulfuric acid solution, and stirred at 60 °C for 4 h. Then, the acidified poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were dispersed in 48 mL of dichloromethane, and 3.6 mL of triethylamine and 3.6 mL of 2-bromoisobutyl bromide, and 20 mg of 4-dimethylaminopyridine were added under ice-bath conditions. Stirring was continued at room temperature for 24 h. The product was washed with dichloromethane, deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, respectively, and dried under vacuum.

Finally, CD@MIP was prepared based on the SI-ATRP reaction. The 0.5 g of modified poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were dispersed in 15 mL of ethanol. Sequentially, 25 mg of Bpy, 0.5 mmol of MCPA and 0.75 mmol of 4-VP and 1 mL of concentrated CD solution were added to sonicate the solution, afterwords, 3 mmol of EDMA was added. The system was quickly sealed and nitrogen was passed through a double row of tubes, followed by a cycle of freezing - evacuating - thawing, which was repeated 3 times. After a quick addition of 25 mg CuBr to seal the system under vented nitrogen, the process of freezing-evacuating-thawing was repeated 3 times. Then the reaction was carried out under nitrogen protection and 60 °C for 8 h. The product was washed sequentially with ethanol, deionized water and EDTA-2Na solution (0.1 mol L−1), dried under vacuum, and MCPA was removed by Soxhlet extraction for 12 h by eluting the product with glacial acetic acid-ethanol mixture (20/80, v/v) as eluent. After drying, the product was acquired and assigned as CD@MIP.
Similarly, MIP was also prepared without the addition of CD solution, and NIP was prepared without the addition of CD solution and MCPA.

The functional groups of the materials were analyzed on a Fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Nicolet, Wisconsin, USA), while elemental analysis (EA) was performed on a UNICUBE (Elementar, GER). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions and specific surface areas were analyzed with a Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET, Tri star 3020, USA) to analyze the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution and specific surface area of the materials. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was carried out on a Discovery TGA 550 (TA, USA), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to observe the morphology of the materials (Philips, Netherlands). The remaining concentration in the MCPA solution was analyzed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV, TU-1950, China), and the fluorescence properties of the materials were determined on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan).

The adsorption capacity of the materials for MCPA was explored by static adsorption experiments. The 10 mg of CD@MIP, MIP or NIP were accurately weighed and dispersed in 100 mL of aqueous MCPA solution at different concentrations. The aqueous MCPA solution was shaken at room temperature and 200 rpm for 150 min and then filtered through a polyethersulfone microporous membrane (0.45 μm). The concentration of MCPA in the filtrate was analyzed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer at λ = 277 nm, and the equilibrium adsorption was calculated by applying equation (1).
(1)
Q e=(C o–C e) × V/m
where Q e is the adsorbed amount of MCPA after adsorption equilibrium, C 0 is the initial concentration of MCPA solution, C e is the equilibrium concentration of MCPA solution, V is the volume of aqueous MCPA solution, and m is the mass of the material.

The relationship graph, i.e. isothermal adsorption curve, was plotted using the initial concentration of MCPA solution, C 0 as the horizontal coordinate and the adsorption capacity of the corresponding concentration of MCPA solution, Q e as the vertical coordinate. The isothermal adsorption curves were analyzed using the Langmuir model equation (2) and Freundlich model equation (3).
(2)
C e/Q e = 1/Q max K L + C e/Q max
(3) lgQ e = lgK F + lgC e/n
where Q max is the maximum adsorption capacity, K L is the adsorption constant of Langmuir model and K F is the adsorption constant of Freundlich model.

As a result of the similar structure of 2,4-D and TPA with MCPA, single sample adsorption experiments were designed. The adsorption capacity of CD@MIP for MCPA was evaluated by comparing the adsorption capacity of 2,4-D and TPA with that of MCPA. A solution of MCPA, 2,4-D and TPA (20 mg L−1) was prepared in 100 mL each, and then 10 mg of CD@MIP were weighed and dispersed into MCPA, 2,4-D and TPA solutions respectively, and then shaken at 200 rpm for 120 min at room temperature. The shaken solutions were filtered through polyethersulfone membrane (0.45 μm). The concentrations of MCPA (λ = 277 nm), 2,4-D (λ = 283 nm) or TPA (λ = 297 nm) were analyzed with a UV–visible spectrophotometer. Equilibrium adsorption was calculated by equation (1).

The rate of adsorption of MCPA by the materials was investigated by dynamic adsorption experiments. Several portions of 10 mg of CD@MIP, MIP or NIP were accurately weighed, dispersed in 100 mL of aqueous MCPA solution at a concentration of 40 mg L−1, and then shaken at 200 rpm for 5, 10, 20, 30, 60, 90, 120 min. The solution was then filtered through polyethersulfone membrane (0.45 μm), and MCPA concentration was analyzed by UV–visible spectrophotometer. Equilibrium adsorption was calculated by equation (1).

The relationship graph, i.e., kinetic adsorption curve, was plotted using the initial concentration of MCPA solution, C 0, as the horizontal coordinate and the adsorption capacity of MCPA, Q e, as the vertical coordinate. The kinetic adsorption curves were analyzed using pseudo-first-order model equation (4) and pseudo-second-order model equation (5).
(4) ln(Q e-Q t) = lnQ e-k 1 t
(5)
t/Q t = t/Q e+1/k 2 Q e 2
where k 1 and k 2 are the rate constants of the quasi-primary and quasi-secondary kinetic modelling equations, respectively, and Q t is the adsorption capacity for MCPA at a given time point.

Several portions of 9 mL of 0–80 μmol L−1 MCPA solution were configured in a 10 mL volumetric flask, and then 1 mL of CD suspension was added to each and shaken well, and left to stand for 5 min. Fluorescence intensity of the solutions was then analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the procedure was repeated 3 times. The optimum excitation wavelength for CD was chosen to be 308 nm, and emission wavelength was 417 nm. The MCPA solution was configured in the same procedures, and 1 mL of CD@MIP suspension was added to the volumetric flask and shaken well. The concentration was analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer after standing for 5 min and repeated 3 times. The degree of fluorescence quenching of MCPA by the material was calculated using the Stern-Volmer equation and equation (6) as described below:
(6)
F 0 / F = 1 + K sv [c]
where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity without the addition of MCPA, F is the fluorescence intensity after the addition of MCPA quenching, K sv is the fluorescence quenching constant, and c is the concentration of MCPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of MCPA in real samples by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: Cabbage purchased from a nearby supermarket (Yinchuan, China). Appropriate amount of crushed cabbage was placed in three centrifuge tubes and MCPA solution (solvent as mobile phase) was added to the centrifuge tubes at concentrations of 1.5, 3 or 4.5 μg L−1 respectively. It was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, passed through polyethersulfone membrane (0.22 μm), and the resulting filtrate was the actual sample of spiked treatment, which was stored under refrigerated conditions. The standard sample was a 30 μg L−1 solution of MCPA. After weighed CD@MIP (60 mg) and loaded into the SPE column, activate the column sequentially by passing 3 mL each of methanol and water through the SPE column. After that, 10 mL of spiked actual sample was passed through the SPE column and washed with 5 mL of water. The sample was eluted with 5 mL of methanol and glacial acetic acid (60/40, v/v), and the eluate was collected and concentrated to 0.5 mL, and the filtrate was analyzed by HPLC. The flow rate was controlled at about 1 mL min−1 during the sample passage and elution.

The HPLC system consisted of an SPD-20A UV–Vis detector and two LC-20-AT pumps (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Chromatographic conditions: chromatographic column, C18 column (ODS, 250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm); mobile phase, methanol-aqueous phosphoric acid (65/35, v/v) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1; injection volume, 10 μL; column temperature, 25 °C; UV wavelength of MCPA, 277 nm.

The preparation process of CD@MIP is shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, monodisperse poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres containing a large number of epoxy functional groups on the surface were prepared according to the method previously reported by our group [29]. The microspheres were modified by acidification and bromination, serving as an initiator for SI-ATRP reaction. As the template molecule MCPA contains a carboxyl group, we chose the basic 4-VP as the functional monomer. Blue fluorescent CD was synthesized according to previous report [28], which could successfully bind with MCPA because they contain a large number of amino groups. Composite CD@MIP was prepared via SI-ATRP technique. Therefore, integration of MIP with CD made CD@MIP more selective and efficient, thus providing a significant advantage in the detection of trace substances in matrices [30–32]. To verify whether the template molecule MCPA was completely eluted, we analyzed the eluent after Soxhlet extraction on HPLC. As shown in Fig. S1, no characteristic peak of MCPA appeared in the chromatogram, proving that MCPA was completely removed.

CD@MIP, MIP and pristine poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were characterized by SEM. The monodisperse poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microsphere is shown in Fig. 2 A and B. It could be observed that the diameter of the microsphere was about 5.6 μm. As shown in Fig. 2C and D, the surface of MIP became slightly rougher but their size almost remained. It was evident that the size and morphology of CD@MIP were almost unchanged (Fig. 2E and F), compared with MIP. These results demonstrated that nanoscale CD had little effect on the morphology of the coated microspheres. The morphology and particle size of CD were characterized by TEM (as shown in Fig. S2). It was observed that the CD particles were circular, with a particle size of about 2 nm. The lattice structure could be clearly observed, confirming the successful preparation of the surface CD.

The specific surface areas of CD@MIP, MIP and NIP were analyzed by nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiment, as shown in Fig. 3 A. It is clear that the amount of nitrogen adsorbed was relatively flat when the relative pressure was less than 0.80. When the relative pressure was higher than 0.8, the nitrogen adsorption increased dramatically, indicating that all three materials possessed abundant macropores, which was helpful for improvement of mass transfer rate. The pore size distribution is shown in Fig. 3B, and the specific surface area, pore volume and average pore size of monodisperse poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres and CD@MIP and MIP are listed in Table 1 . The specific surface area of monodisperse poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres was 23.4 m2 g−1, that of CD@MIP was further decreased to 16.1 cm2 g−1, and the pore volume of them was less than 0.1 cm3 g−1. As a result, poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres and CD@MIP and MIP possessed somewhat mesopores, but were lack of micropores.

The functional groups of CD@MIP, MIP and poly (GMA-co-EDMA) were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 3C. The characteristic peak of epoxy group appeared at 909 cm−1, which did not appear in the spectra of both MIP and CD@MIP, indicating that the epoxy group was protonated during acidification. The characteristic peak of bromine appeared at 669 cm−1, demonstrating successful grafting of bromine in the poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres. The –CN group stretching vibration peak at 1455 cm−1 indicated successful integration of 4-VP into the material. In CD@MIP spectrum, a stretching vibration peak of –CO group at 1731 cm−1 and -C-O group at 1155 cm−1 could be observed, indicating the successful polymerization of EDMA. Finally, the peak at 3500 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibration of –NH group, also proving the successful introduction of CD into CD@MIP. The elemental contents of monodisperse CD@MIP, MIP and pristine poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were measured, and the data are shown in Table S1. Compared to pristine poly (GMA-co-EDMA) (C: 48.61%, H: 6.83%, N: 0.38%) microspheres, MIP (N: 1.41%) and CD@MIP (N: 1.64%) showed a significant increase of the nitrogen content, demonstrating successful integration of 4-VP into imprinting layers of MIP and CD@MIP. Whereas an increase in the elemental content of C (C: 51.67%), H (H: 7.45%) indicated successful polymer preparation, meanwhile, the content of each element of CD@MIP (C: 52.78%, H: 7.98%, N:1.64%) was higher than that of MIP, which indicated the successful doped of CD. The thermal stability of poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres, CD@MIP and MIP was characterized by TG analysis, and the weight loss of them from 26 to 800 °C is displayed in Fig. 3D. It was evident that there was almost no weight loss up to the temperature of 270 °C, suggesting that all of three materials were thermally stable. In the temperature interval of 270–600 °C, the mass of all three materials was remarkably decreased, and no more weight loss was observed after 600 °C. The higher weight loss of CD@MIP (weight: 16.1%) and MIP (weight: 15.0%), compared to monodisperse poly (GMA-co-EDMA) (weight: 21.8%) microspheres, was attributed to the large amount of collapsed imprinted layer covering the surfaces of CD@MIP and MIP. The weight loss of CD@MIP is slightly higher than that of MIP, probably due to the doping of CD.

The determination of the zeta potential of a suspension assessed the stability of MIP and CD@MIP in aqueous solution. Generally speaking, the larger value of the zeta potential of a suspension, the more stable the suspension system. The zeta potentials of MIP and CD@MIP suspensions at a concentration of 10 mg L−1 were measured. The average zeta potential of MIP was measured to be −71.4 mV, indicating excellent stability of MIP in water. However, the average zeta potential of CD@MIP was −59.3 mV, indicating that the addition of CD reduced the stability of the material in water.

The amount of adsorption to the template was a key reflection of the material's performance, which was determined using saturation adsorption experiments. The isothermal adsorption curve is shown in Fig. 4 A. It was obvious that the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent for MCPA gradually increased in the range of 10–50 mg L−1, and the adsorption reached saturation equilibrium at 50 mg L−1 of MCPA. The maximum adsorption capacity of CD@MIP and MIP for MCPA was 93.9 and 97.1 mg g−1, respectively, both of which were much higher than that of NIP for MCPA (36.4 mg g−1). It was attributed to the fact that CD@MIP and MIP possessed imprinted cavities selectively recognized for MCPA, whereas NIP possessed non-imprinted cavity. MIP and CD@MIP exhibited specific binding capabilities to MCPA, with imprinting factor (IF) values of 2.7 and 2.6, respectively, which was defined as the ratio of saturated adsorption between MIP and NIP, reflecting the binding capacity of MIP compared to NIP [33,34].

In order to investigate the adsorption mechanism of MCPA by the materials, isothermal adsorption curves were analyzed using Langmuir model equation (2) and Freundlich model equation (3). The results are shown in Table S2 and Fig. S3. The linear correlation coefficients of MIP (0.997), CD@MIP (0.995) and NIP (0.994) were acquired for the Langmuir model, while those of MIP (0.959), CD@MIP (0.941) and NIP (0.986) were obtained for Freundlich model, respectively. Apparently, the adsorption mechanism of MCPA by CD@MIP, MIP and NIP was more consistent with the Langmuir model. The model assumes adsorption in a monolayer adsorption mode on a homogeneous surface with maximum adsorption capacity.

Since there was little difference from the adsorption capacity of CD@MIP and MIP for MCPA, CD@MIP was chosen for adsorption of MCPA as well as the structurally similar 2,4-D and TPA. The specific recognition of MCPA by CD@MIP was evaluated by comparison of adsorption amounts (Fig. S4). It was evident that the adsorption of CD@MIP on MCPA (60.5 mg g−1) was remarkably higher than those of 2,4-D (27.6 mg g−1) and TPA (10.9 mg g−1). It was deduced that the imprinted cavities on the surface of material were complementary to the size and shape of MCPA molecule as well as the functional groups. In contrast, the adsorption of 2,4-D by CD@MIP was slightly higher than that of TPA, probably due to the fact that the molecular structure of 2,4-D was more similar to that of MCPA.

The rate of adsorption of MCPA on the materials can be evaluated by adsorption kinetics experiment, and the kinetic adsorption curves are presented in Fig. 4B. It could be seen that the adsorption of MCPA by the material increased sharply in the initial 20 min, and the adsorption of MCPA tended to equilibrate at about 30 min. The saturation adsorption of MCPA by CD@MIP and MIP was 82.6 and 80.4 mg g−1, respectively, while that by NIP reached 37.7 mg g−1. Obviously, the adsorption capacity of NIP was much lower than those of CD@MIP and MIP. This was attributed to the fact that the imprinted cavities, which were selectively recognized for MCPA, could bind to the template more efficiently and rapidly than the non-imprinted cavities, and therefore possessed superior adsorption rate. The gradual decrease of adsorption rate may be due to the fact that there were fewer cavities on the surface of the adsorbent.

In order to further investigate the mechanism of adsorption kinetics, the kinetic adsorption curves were fitted using the pseudo-first-order kinetic model equation (4) and the pseudo-second-order kinetic model equation (5). In general, the pseudo-first-order kinetic model assumes that the rate of adsorption depends on the rate of diffusion while the pseudo-first-order kinetic model assumes that it is the chemical force that plays a decisive role in the rate of adsorption. The linear correlation coefficients of MIP (0.837), CD@MIP (0.795) and NIP (0.895) were acquired for the pseudo-first-order, while those of MIP (0.999), CD@MIP (0.999) and NIP (0.998) were obtained for pseudo-second-order, respectively, as shown in Table S3 and Fig. S5. Apparent, adsorbents were more in line with the pseudo-second-order kinetic adsorption model, which describes the process of chemical adsorption.

The adsorption amounts of MCPA with other reported materials are listed in Table 2 . Mohammadi prepared magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs-NH2) by co-precipitation method with an adsorption capacity of 153.5 mg g−1 for MCPA, reaching an equilibrium at 60 min [35]. Niaz utilized biochar generated under high-temperature pyrolysis (WPRAW), which exhibited an adsorption capacity of 9.5 mg g−1 for MCPA with an equilibrium time of 1440 min [36]. Essandoh prepared Switchgrass biochar using a screw conveyor fast pyrolysis reactor with an adsorption of 38.1 mg g−1 for MCPA and an equilibrium time of 360 min [37]. Andrunik prepared zeolite-carbon composites by a one-step hydrothermal conversion method, which adsorbed MCPA as low as 0.7 mg g−1 [38]. Santiago prepared montmorillonite by particle exchange method, which could adsorb MCPA of 60 mg g−1 with an equilibrium time of 720 min [39]. In our case, the CD@MIP not only exhibited a larger saturated adsorption amount but also quickly reached the adsorption equilibrium, compared with other adsorbent materials. Although the adsorption capacity of MNPs-NH2 nanoparticles was higher than that of CD@MIP, the adsorption equilibrium time was longer.

In order to verify the quenching performance of the prepared blue fluorescent CD against MCPA, the fluorescence quenching of CD by MCPA was determined in the concentration range of 0–80 μmol L−1. The fluorescence spectra of CD and CD@MIP quenching by MCPA are shown in Fig. 5 A and C. It could be observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly with an increase of MCPA concentration. The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity versus MCPA concentration at 5–80 μmol L−1 is presented in Fig. 5B and D, indicating that CD and CD@MIP show a good linear relationship with the concentration of MCPA in the range of 0–80 μmol L−1 (R = 0.993 of CD and R = 0.996 of CD@MIP). There was a difference when CD grafted on MIP changed the excitation (290 nm) and emission (389 nm) wavelengths, but had little effect on the fluorescence quenching of MCPA.

The limit of detection (LOD) of CD@MIP was measured to be 3.5 μmol L−1 according to the 3σ method. The quantum yield of CD@MIP was measured by using quinine sulphate as the reference compound, and the quantum yield of CD@MIP was 13.4%. As a result, MCPA possessed more excellent fluorescence burst performance for CD@MIP, with high fluorescence yield and low LOD, and good fluorescence performance, which was suitable for the detection of MCPA.

In this work, the as-synthesized CD contains a large number of amino groups, while MCPA has an electron-rich aromatic ring. As a result, there was a strong charge transfer interaction between the electron-rich aromatic ring and the electron-deficient amino group. MCPA has an electron-rich aromatic ring, and the transfer of a large number of electrons to electron-deficient amino group on the surface of CD leads to the formation of a Meisenheimer complex with the carboxyl group of MCPA, during which the energy of CD was transferred to the complex, resulting in the CD fluorescence quenching. In addition, molecular orbital theory could also interpret the fluorescence quenching mechanism. Under the irradiation of UV light, the electrons in CD were excited from valence band to conduction band and then returned to ground state, and then the fluorescence was emitted in the process of returning to the ground state. Hydrogen bonds were generated between the carboxyl group of MCPA and the amino group of CD, and this strong intermolecular force makes the electron transfer between CD@MIP and MCPA. Electrons that were excited to the conduction band could easily jump from CD to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of complex during their return to the ground state. Due to higher energy level of the complex, it was more favorable for the electron to leap to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the complex rather than the ground state molecular orbital of CD. As a result, the electron would remain in that orbital, which leads to a fluorescence quenching [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of CD@MIP in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we plotted relationship between the concentration of MCPA as horizontal coordinate and the chromatographic peak area after HPLC analysis as vertical coordinate. The linear equation was obtained as y = 741.3x+123.5 (R = 0.999), which exhibited a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 0.02–15 μg mL−1. The LOD was 6 μg L−1 based on the three-fold signal-to-noise ratio (S/N), and the limit of quantification (LOQ) was 20 μg L−1 based on the ten-fold S/N. Subsequently, the enrichment ability of CD@MIP for MCPA was explored by SPE, and the eluent was analyzed on HPLC. Fig. 6 A presents chromatogram of the standard sample as 30 μg L−1‵for MCPA, Fig. 6B presents chromatogram of actual cabbage sample, while Fig. 6C shows chromatogram of the spiked actual cabbage sample after CD@MIP enrichment. We verified the enrichment ability of CD@MIP by enriching trace amounts of MCPA and comparing the chromatographic peaks obtained after HPLC analysis with those of standard solution. The concentration of MCPA in actual cabbage sample was 1.5 μg L−1, which reached 28.6 μg L−1 after enriched by CD@MIP. The result met our expectations. The recoveries were 90.8%–95.3%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD%, n = 3) was 2.4%. In conclusion, CD@MIP possessed an excellent enrichment ability for MCPA.

In order to evaluate the reliability of the material, we designed intra-day and inter-day experiments, and three spiked actual samples with concentrations of 1.5, 3, and 4.5 μg kg−1 were selected to evaluate the precision of the material, and three measurements were made in one day and one measurement was made in each of the 3 days, and the results are shown in Table S4. Intra-day recovery was 91.3%–97.1% with an RSD (n = 3) of 1.5%–4.7%, and inter-day recovery was 90.5%–98% with an RSD (n = 3) of 2.1%–5.9%. It could be seen that the higher recoveries and lower RSD could prove that CD@MIP had excellent enrichment performance for MCPA, and the material was highly reliable and precise, and its application to the enrichment of MCPA in the environment was very promising.

In this study, a blue fluorescent CD, which exhibited quenching effect on MCPA, was fabricated and then integrated into MIP-modified microspheres via SI-ATRP. CD@MIP exhibits several advantages. Firstly, under the same conditions, the adsorption performance of CD@MIP was almost identical to that of MIP, far superior to the NIP. Secondly, CD@MIP was capable of fluorescently detecting MCPA, exhibiting satisfactory linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and MCPA concentration in the range of 0–80 μmol L−1. Lastly, the enrichment ability of CD@MIP for MCPA in real samples was validated through SPE and HPLC, confirming its practical application prospect. In summary, CD@MIP combined the specific adsorption capability of MIP with the fluorescent ability of CD, enabling simultaneous enrichment and detection of MCPA in vegetables. It is expected that dual-functional MIP integrating molecular imprinting technology and photoluminescence functionality hold broad development potential, offering potentials in fields such as food safety and environmental monitoring.

Haiping Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yi Xu: Supervision, Formal analysis. Qingyan Bai: Investigation. Shujuan Ma: Validation. Chunmiao Bo: Validation. Junjie Ou: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interestsw or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support is gratefully acknowledged from the CAS-Weigao Research & Development Program ([2017]-009) and the National Natural Sciences Foundation of Ningxia (No. 2021AAC02017) to J. Ou.
